subject: Math
description: Partition circles and rectangles into two and four equal shares. Describe the shares using the words, halves, fourths, and quarters and phrases half of, fourth of and quarter of. Describe the whole as two of or four of the shares. Understand for these examples that decomposing (break apart) into more equal shares creates smaller shares.
description_plain: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into two or four equal parts and use words like halves, fourths, and quarters to describe them, understanding that more parts mean smaller pieces.

subject: Math
description: Compose (put together) two-dimensional or three-dimensional shapes to create a larger, composite shape, and compose new shapes from the composite shape.
description_plain: Your child can put together flat or solid shapes to make bigger shapes and then create new shapes from those.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish between defining attributes (e.g., triangles are closed and three-sided) versus non-defining attributes. Identify shapes that have non-defining attributes (e.g., color, orientation, overall size). Build and draw shapes given specified attributes.
description_plain: Your child understands the important features that define shapes, like a triangle having three sides, and can build and draw shapes showing those features.

subject: Math
description: Organize, represent and interpret data with up to three categories. Ask and answer comparison and quantity questions about the data.
description_plain: Your child can organize and understand data with up to three groups and ask or answer questions comparing the amounts.

subject: Math
description: Identify values of coins (e.g., nickel = 5 cents, quarter = 25 cents). Identify equivalent values of coins up to $1 (e.g., 5 pennies = 1 nickel, 5 nickels = 1 quarter).
description_plain: Your child can recognize coin values like nickels and quarters and know how different coins add up to the same amount, up to one dollar.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and read money symbols including $ and ¢.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and read money symbols like the dollar sign ($) and cents sign (¢).

subject: Math
description: Read a calendar distinguishing yesterday, today and tomorrow. Read and write a date.
description_plain: Your child can read a calendar to tell yesterday, today, and tomorrow and can read and write dates.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time in half hours using both analog and digital clocks.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the half hour using both clocks with hands and digital clocks.

subject: Math
description: Express the length of an object as a whole number of length units, by laying multiple copies of a shorter object (the length unit) end to end; understand that the length measurement of an object is the number of same-size length units that span it with no gaps or overlaps.
description_plain: Your child can measure how long something is by lining up smaller objects of the same size without gaps or overlaps and count how many it takes.

subject: Math
description: Measure and compare three objects using standard or non-standard units.
description_plain: Your child can measure and compare the length of three objects using either standard or everyday items as units.

subject: Math
description:
Subtract multiples of 10 up to 100. Use:
  • concrete models or drawings
  • strategies based on place value
  • properties of operations
  • and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.
Relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can subtract multiples of 10 up to 100 using drawings, place value ideas, or addition and subtraction facts, and explain how they solved it.

subject: Math
description: Given a two-digit number, mentally find 10 more or 10 less than the number, without having to count; explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can quickly find 10 more or 10 less than any two-digit number and explain how they know.

subject: Math
description:
Add using numbers up to 100 including adding a two-digit number and a one-digit number and adding a two-digit number and a multiple of 10. Use:
  • concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value
  • properties of operations
  • and/or relationship between addition and subtraction.
Relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used. Demonstrate in adding two-digit numbers, tens and tens are added, ones and ones are added and sometimes it is necessary to compose a ten from ten ones.
description_plain: Your child can add numbers up to 100, including adding two-digit and one-digit numbers or multiples of 10, using drawings or place value ideas and explain their thinking.

subject: Math
description: Compare two two-digit numbers based on meanings of the tens and ones digits, recording the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, <.
description_plain: Your child can compare two two-digit numbers by looking at their tens and ones and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: The numbers 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight or nine tens (and 0 ones).
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers like 10, 20, 30, up to 90 mean one to nine groups of ten and zero ones.

subject: Math
description: The numbers from 11 to 19 are composed of a ten and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight or nine ones.
description_plain: Your child knows that numbers from 11 to 19 are made of one ten and some ones from one to nine.

subject: Math
description: 10 can be thought of as a bundle of ten ones, called a "ten".
description_plain: Your child understands that 10 is a group of ten ones bundled together called a 'ten'.

subject: Math
description: Model and identify place value positions of two digit numbers. Include:
description_plain: Your child can show and identify the place value of digits in two-digit numbers, like tens and ones.

subject: Math
description: Count to 120. In this range, read, write and order numerals and represent a number of objects with a written numeral.
description_plain: Your child can count to 120, read and write numbers in this range, and match numbers to groups of objects.

subject: Math
description: Identify, continue and label patterns (e.g., aabb, abab). Create patterns using number, shape, size, rhythm or color.
description_plain: Your child can find, continue, and name patterns using numbers, shapes, sizes, colors, or rhythms.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unknown whole number in an addition or subtraction equation.
description_plain: Your child can figure out the missing number in addition or subtraction problems.

subject: Math
description: Understand the meaning of the equal sign (e.g., read equal sign as "same as") and determine if equations involving addition and subtraction are true or false.
description_plain: Your child understands that the equal sign means 'the same as' and can tell if addition or subtraction equations are true or false.

subject: Math
description:
Add and subtract using numbers up to 20, demonstrating fluency for addition and subtraction up to 10. Use strategies such as
  • counting on
  • making ten(8 + 6 = 8 + 2 + 4 = 10 + 4 = 14)
  • decomposing a number leading to a ten (13 - 4 = 13 - 3 - 1 = 10 - 1 = 9)
  • using the relationship between addition and subtraction, such as fact families, (8 + 4 = 12 and 12 - 8 = 4)
  • creating equivalent but easier or known sums (e.g., adding 6 + 7 by creating the known equivalent 6 + 6 + 1 = 12 + 1 = 13).
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 20, using strategies like counting on, making ten, breaking numbers apart, and using related addition and subtraction facts.

subject: Math
description: Relate counting to addition and subtraction (e.g., by counting on 2 to add 2).
description_plain: Your child can use counting to help add or subtract numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand subtraction as an unknown-addend problem.
description_plain: Your child understands subtraction as finding the missing addend in an addition problem.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract. (Students need not know the name of the property.)
description_plain: Your child can use properties of addition and subtraction to help solve problems, even if they don't know the names of these properties.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems that call for addition of three whole numbers whose sum is less than or equal to 20 (e.g., by using objects, drawings and equations). Record and explain using equation symbols and a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add three whole numbers with a total of 20 or less.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction strategies to solve word problems (using numbers up to 20), involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart and comparing, with unknowns in all positions, using a number line (e.g., by using objects, drawings and equations). Record and explain using equation symbols and a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems using addition and subtraction with numbers up to 20, using objects, drawings, number lines, and equations with unknown numbers.

subject: Math
description: Estimate how many and how much in a given set to 20 and then verify estimate by counting.
description_plain: Your child can guess how many items are in a group up to 20 and then count to check the guess.

subject: Math
description: Use the symbols for greater than, less than or equal to when comparing two numbers or groups of objects.
description_plain: Your child can use >, <, and = symbols to compare two numbers or groups of objects.

subject: Math
description: Count a large quantity of objects by grouping into 10s and counting by 10s and 1s to find the quantity.
description_plain: Your child can count large groups by making groups of ten and then counting by tens and ones.

subject: Math
description: Order numbers from 1-100. Demonstrate ability in counting forward and backward.
description_plain: Your child can put numbers from 1 to 100 in order and count forwards and backwards.

subject: Math
description: Use ordinal numbers correctly when identifying object position (e.g., first, second, third, etc.).
description_plain: Your child can use words like first, second, and third to show the position of objects.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Partition circles and rectangles into shares, describe the shares using the words halves, thirds, half of, a third of, etc., and describe the whole as two halves, three thirds, four fourths. Recognize that equal shares of identical wholes need not have the same shape.
description_plain: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into equal parts like halves and thirds, describe these parts, and understand that equal parts can look different.

subject: Math
description: Partition a rectangle into rows and columns of same-size squares and count to find the total number of them.
description_plain: Your child can divide a rectangle into rows and columns of equal squares and count how many squares there are.

subject: Math
description: Identify and draw shapes having specified attributes, such as a given number of angles or a given number of equal faces compared visually, not by measuring. Identify triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons and cubes.
description_plain: Your child can identify and draw shapes by their features, like how many angles or equal sides they have, including triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and cubes.

subject: Math
description: Draw a picture graph and a bar graph (with single-unit scale) to represent a data set with up to four categories. Solve simple put-together, take-apart and compare problems using information presented in a bar graph.
description_plain: Your child can draw picture and bar graphs with up to four groups and use them to solve simple problems about adding, taking away, or comparing amounts.

subject: Math
description: Collect, record, interpret, represent, and describe data in a table, graph or line plot.
description_plain: Your child can collect, record, and explain data using tables, graphs, or line plots.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving dollar bills and coins using the $ and ¢ symbols appropriately.
description_plain: Your child can solve money word problems using dollar bills and coins and use the $ and ¢ symbols correctly.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time to the nearest five minutes using a.m. and p.m. from analog and digital clocks.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write the time to the nearest five minutes using both a.m. and p.m. on analog and digital clocks.

subject: Math
description: Represent whole numbers as lengths from 0 on a number line diagram with equally spaced points corresponding to the numbers 0, 1,2, …, and represent whole-number sums and differences within 100 on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can show whole numbers on a number line starting at zero and use the number line to add and subtract numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Solve addition and subtraction word problems using numbers up to 100 involving length that are given in the same units (e.g., by using drawings of rulers). Write an equation with a symbol for the unknown to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems about length using addition and subtraction with numbers up to 100, and write equations with a symbol for the unknown number.

subject: Math
description: Measure to compare lengths of two objects, expressing the difference in terms of a standard length unit.
description_plain: Your child can measure and compare the lengths of two objects and say how much longer or shorter one is using standard units.

subject: Math
description: Estimate, measure and draw lengths using whole units of inches, feet, yards, centimeters and meters.
description_plain: Your child can estimate, measure, and draw lengths using whole units like inches, feet, yards, centimeters, and meters.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object twice using different length units for the two measurements. Describe how the two measurements relate to the size of the unit chosen.
description_plain: Your child can measure the same object using two different units and explain how the measurements change because of the unit size.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object by selecting and using standard tools such as rulers, yardsticks, meter sticks, and measuring tapes.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use tools like rulers, yardsticks, meter sticks, or measuring tapes to measure the length of objects.

subject: Math
description: Explain or illustrate the processes of addition or subtraction and their relationship using place value and the properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can explain or show how addition and subtraction work using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Mentally add 10 or 100 to a given number 100-900 and mentally subtract 10 or 100 from a given number.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add or subtract 10 or 100 to numbers between 100 and 900 in their head.

subject: Math
description:
Add and subtract using numbers up to 1000. Use:
  • concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value
  • properties of operations
  • and/or relationship between addition and subtraction.
Relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used. Demonstrate in adding or subtracting three-digit numbers, hundreds and hundreds are added or subtracted, tens and tens are added or subtracted, ones and ones are added or subtracted and sometimes it is necessary to compose a ten from ten ones or a hundred from ten tens.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 1000 using drawings, place value, and math rules, and explain how they solve the problems step-by-step.

subject: Math
description: Add up to four two-digit numbers using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can add up to four two-digit numbers using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description:
Fluently add and subtract using numbers up to 100. Use:
  • strategies based on place value
  • properties of operations
  • and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 100 using place value, math rules, and the connection between addition and subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Compare two three-digit numbers based on the meanings of the hundreds, tens and ones digits, using >, =, < symbols to record the results.
description_plain: Your child can compare two three-digit numbers by looking at the hundreds, tens, and ones digits and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Read, write, order up to 1000 using base-ten numerals, number names and expanded form.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and put in order numbers up to 1000 using digits, words, and expanded form.

subject: Math
description: Count up to 1000, skip-count by 5s, 10s and 100s.
description_plain: Your child can count to 1000 and skip count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.

subject: Math
description: The numbers 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine hundreds (and 0 tens and 0 ones).
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers like 100, 200, 300, up to 900 mean one to nine hundreds with zero tens and ones.

subject: Math
description: 100 can be thought of as a bundle of ten tens --called a "hundred".
description_plain: Your child understands that 100 is a group of ten tens, which is called a 'hundred.'

subject: Math
description: Model and identify place value positions of three digit numbers. Include:
description_plain: Your child can show and understand the place value of hundreds, tens, and ones in three-digit numbers.

subject: Math
description: Identify, continue and label number patterns (e.g., aabb, abab). Describe a rule that determines and continues a sequence or pattern.
description_plain: Your child can find, continue, and name number patterns and explain the rule that makes the pattern continue.

subject: Math
description: Use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rectangular arrays with up to 5 rows and up to 5 columns. Write an equation to express the total as repeated addition (e.g., array of 4 by 5 would be 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 20).
description_plain: Your child can use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rows and columns (up to 5 rows and 5 columns) and write an equation showing equal groups added together.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether a group of objects (up to 20) is odd or even (e.g., by pairing objects and comparing, counting by 2s). Model an even number as two equal groups of objects and then write an equation as a sum of two equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can tell if a group of up to 20 objects is odd or even by pairing or counting by twos, and write an equation showing two equal groups for even numbers.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract using numbers up to 20 using mental strategies. Know from memory all sums of two one-digit numbers.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 20 in their head and knows all sums of two one-digit numbers from memory.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction strategies to estimate, then solve one- and two-step word problems (using numbers up to 100) involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart and comparing, with unknowns in all positions (e.g., by using objects, drawings and equations). Record and explain using equation symbols and a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can use addition and subtraction to estimate and solve one- and two-step word problems with numbers up to 100, using objects, drawings, and equations with an unknown number.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Partition shapes into parts with equal areas. Express the area of each part as a unit fraction of the whole.
description_plain: Your child can divide shapes into equal parts and say the area of each part as a fraction of the whole shape.

subject: Math
description: Categorize shapes by different attribute classifications and recognize that shared attributes can define a larger category. Generalize to create examples or non-examples.
description_plain: Your child can sort shapes by their features and understand that shapes with shared features belong to bigger groups, and can give examples or non-examples.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions with the same numerator or the same denominator by reasoning about their size. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, or <, and justify the conclusions (e.g., by using a visual fraction model).
description_plain: Your child can compare two fractions that have the same top number or the same bottom number by thinking about their size, knowing the fractions must be parts of the same whole.

subject: Math
description: Express and model whole numbers as fractions, and recognize and construct fractions that are equivalent to whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can show whole numbers as fractions and find fractions that are equal to whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and generate simple equivalent fractions (e.g., 1/2 = 2/4, 4/6 = 2/3). Explain why the fractions are equivalent (e.g., by using a visual fraction model).
description_plain: Your child can find and explain simple fractions that are equal, like 1/2 and 2/4, using pictures or models.

subject: Math
description: Understand two fractions as equivalent if they are the same size (modeled) or the same point on a number line.
description_plain: Your child understands that two fractions are equal if they are the same size or at the same spot on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Explain equivalence of fractions in special cases, and compare fractions by reasoning about their size.
description_plain: Your child can explain when fractions are equal and compare fractions by thinking about their size.

subject: Math
description: Represent a fraction a/b (e.g., 2/8) on a number line diagram or ruler by marking off a lengths 1/b (e.g., 1/8) from 0. Recognize that the resulting interval has size a/b (e.g., 2/8) and that its endpoint locates the number a/b (e.g., 2/8) on the number line.
description_plain: Your child can show a fraction like 2/8 on a number line by marking off equal parts and understand where that fraction is located.

subject: Math
description: Represent a fraction 1/b (e.g., 1/4) on a number line diagram by defining the interval from 0 to 1 as the whole and partitioning it into b (e.g., 4) equal parts. Recognize that each part has size 1/b (e.g., 1/4) and that the endpoint of the part based at 0 locates the number 1/b (e.g., 1/4) on the number line.
description_plain: Your child can show a fraction like 1/4 on a number line by dividing the line from 0 to 1 into equal parts and find where 1/4 is located.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction as a number on the number line; represent fractions on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child understands fractions as numbers on a number line and can show fractions on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction 1/b (e.g., 1/4) as the quantity formed by 1 part when a whole is partitioned into b (e.g., 4) equal parts; understand a fraction a/b (e.g., 2/4) as the quantity formed by a (e.g., 2) parts of size 1/b. (e.g., 1/4)
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction like 1/4 means one part when a whole is split into four equal parts, and a fraction like 2/4 means two of those parts.

subject: Math
description:
Solve real world and mathematical problems involving perimeters of polygons, including:
  • finding the perimeter given the side lengths,
  • finding an unknown side length,
  • exhibiting rectangles with the same perimeter and different areas,
  • exhibiting rectangles with the same area and different perimeters.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about the perimeter of shapes, like finding the perimeter when side lengths are known, finding missing side lengths, and showing rectangles with the same perimeter but different areas or the same area but different perimeters.

subject: Math
description: Recognize area as additive. Find areas of rectilinear figures by decomposing them into non-overlapping rectangles and adding the areas of the non-overlapping parts, applying this technique to solve real world problems.
description_plain: Your child knows that area can be found by adding parts together and can find the area of shapes made of rectangles by adding their areas.

subject: Math
description: Use area models (rectangular arrays) to represent the distributive property in mathematical reasoning. Use tiling to show in a concrete case that the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths a and b + c is the sum of a × b and a × c.
description_plain: Your child can use area models to show how multiplication works by breaking a rectangle into parts and adding their areas.

subject: Math
description: Multiply side lengths to find areas of rectangles with whole number side lengths in the context of solving real world and mathematical problems, and represent whole-number products as rectangular areas in mathematical reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of rectangles by multiplying side lengths and use this to solve real problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of a rectangle with whole-number side lengths by tiling it, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of a rectangle by covering it with squares and show that this is the same as multiplying the side lengths.

subject: Math
description: Relate area to the operations of multiplication and addition.
description_plain: Your child understands that area is related to multiplication and addition.

subject: Math
description: Measure areas by tiling with unit squares (square centimeters, square meters, square inches, square feet, and improvised units).
description_plain: Your child can find the area of shapes by covering them with small squares like square centimeters or square inches.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate that a plane figure which can be covered without gaps or overlaps by n (e.g., 6) unit squares is said to have an area of n (e.g., 6) square units.
description_plain: Your child understands that if a flat shape can be covered exactly by a certain number of squares, its area is the same as the number of those squares.

subject: Math
description: A square with side length 1 unit is said to have "one square unit" and can be used to measure area.
description_plain: Your child knows that a square with sides one unit long counts as one square unit to measure area.

subject: Math
description: Recognize area as an attribute of plane figures and understand concepts of area measurement.
description_plain: Your child understands that area is a way to describe the size of flat shapes and learns how to measure it.

subject: Math
description: Explain the classification of data from real-world problems shown in graphical representations. Use the terms minimum and maximum. (L)
description_plain: Your child can explain data shown in graphs and use words like minimum and maximum to describe it.

subject: Math
description: Measure and record lengths using rulers marked with halves and fourths of an inch. Make a line plot with the data, where the horizontal scale is marked off in appropriate units—whole numbers, halves, or quarters.
description_plain: Your child can measure lengths using rulers with halves and quarters of an inch and make line plots to show the measurements.

subject: Math
description: Draw a scaled picture graph and a scaled bar graph to represent a data set with several categories. Solve one- and two-step "how many more" and "how many less" problems using information presented in scaled bar graphs.
description_plain: Your child can draw picture and bar graphs with a scale to show data and solve problems about how much more or less one category has.

subject: Math
description: Select an appropriate unit of English, metric, or non-standard measurement to estimate the length, time, weight, or temperature (L)
description_plain: Your child can choose the right unit to estimate or measure length, time, weight, or temperature.

subject: Math
description: Estimate and measure liquid volumes and masses of objects using standard units of grams (g), kilograms (kg), and liters (l). (Excludes compound units such as cm3 and finding the geometric volume of a container.)Add, subtract, multiply, or divide to solve and create one-step word problems involving masses or volumes that are given in the same units (e.g., by using drawings, such as a beaker with a measurement scale, to represent the problem). (Excludes multiplicative comparison problems [problems involving notions of "times as much."])
description_plain: Your child can estimate and measure how much things weigh or how much liquid they hold using grams, kilograms, and liters, and solve simple math problems with these measurements.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time to the nearest minute and measure time intervals in minutes. Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of time intervals in minutes or hours (e.g., by representing the problem on a number line diagram or clock).
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the nearest minute, measure time intervals, and solve problems adding or subtracting time.

subject: Math
description: Multiply one-digit whole numbers by multiples of 10 in the range 10-90 (e.g., 9 x 80, 10 x 60) using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can multiply one-digit numbers by multiples of 10 (like 9 × 80) using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Use strategies and/or algorithms to fluently add and subtract with numbers up to 1000, demonstrating understanding of place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 1000 easily, using place value and understanding how addition and subtraction work together.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100.
description_plain: Your child can round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100 using place value.

subject: Math
description: Identify arithmetic patterns (including patterns in the addition table or multiplication table) and explain them using properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can find and explain patterns in addition and multiplication tables using math rules.

subject: Math
description: Solve and create two-step word problems using any of the four operations. Represent these problems using equations with a symbol (box, circle, question mark) standing for the unknown quantity. Assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding.
description_plain: Your child can solve and make two-step word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division and check if answers make sense by estimating.

subject: Math
description: Fluently multiply and divide numbers up to 100, using strategies such as the relationship between multiplication and division (e.g., knowing that 8 ×5 = 40, one knows 40 ÷ 5 = 8) or properties of operations. By the end of Grade 3, know from memory all products of two one-digit numbers.
description_plain: Your child can quickly multiply and divide numbers up to 100 and knows all multiplication facts for one-digit numbers by memory.

subject: Math
description: Understand division as an unknown-factor problem.
description_plain: Your child understands division as finding the missing number in a multiplication problem.

subject: Math
description:
Make, test, support, draw conclusions and justify conjectures about properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide. (Students need not use formal terms for these properties.)
  • Commutative property of multiplication: If 6 × 4 = 24 is known, then 4 × 6 = 24 is also known.
  • Associative property of multiplication: 3 × 5 × 2 can be found by 3 × 5 = 15, then 15 × 2 = 30, or by 5 × 2 = 10, then 3 × 10 = 30.
  • Distributive property: Knowing that 8 × 5 = 40 and 8 × 2 = 16, one can find 8 × 7 as 8 × (5 + 2) = (8 × 5) + (8 × 2) = 40 + 16 = 56.
  • Inverse property (relationship) of multiplication and division.
description_plain: Your child can explain and use math rules to help multiply and divide, like switching numbers around or breaking problems into parts.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unknown whole number in a multiplication or division equation relating three whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can find the missing number in multiplication or division equations with three whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use multiplication and division numbers up to 100 to solve word problems in situations involving equal groups, arrays, and measurement quantities (e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem).
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems using multiplication and division with numbers up to 100 by drawing pictures or writing equations.

subject: Math
description: Interpret whole-number quotients of whole numbers (e.g., interpret 56 ÷ 8 as the number of objects in each share when 56 objects are partitioned equally into 8 shares, or as a number of shares when 56 objects are partitioned into equal shares of 8 objects each).
description_plain: Your child understands division as sharing objects equally or grouping them and can explain what the answer means.

subject: Math
description: Interpret products of whole numbers (e.g., interpret 5 × 7 as the total number of objects in 5 groups of 7 objects each).
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication as adding equal groups and can explain what the total means.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Solve or identify solutions to problems involving possible combinations (e.g., if ice cream sundaes come in 3 flavors with 2 possible toppings, how many different sundaes can be made using only one flavor of ice cream with one topping?) (L)
description_plain: Your child can figure out how many different combinations are possible in a situation, like choosing ice cream flavors and toppings.

subject: Math
description: Analyze whether a game is mathematically fair or unfair by explaining the probability of all possible outcomes. (L)
description_plain: Your child can decide if a game is fair by explaining the chances of all possible outcomes.

subject: Math
description: Relating the choice of measures of center and variability to the shape of the data distribution and the context in which the data were gathered.
description_plain: Your child can choose the best way to describe data based on its shape and the situation it came from.

subject: Math
description: Giving quantitative measures of center (median and/or mean) and variability (interquartile range), as well as describing any overall pattern and any outliers with reference to the context in which the data were gathered.
description_plain: Your child can find and explain the middle (median or mean) and spread (range) of data, describe patterns, and point out unusual values.

subject: Math
description: Describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, including how it was measured and its units of measurement.
description_plain: Your child can describe what is being measured in data, how it was measured, and the units used.

subject: Math
description: Reporting the number of observations (occurrences).
description_plain: Your child can tell how many times something was observed or counted in data.

subject: Math
description: Summarize numerical data sets in relation to their context, such as by:
description_plain: Your child can summarize sets of numbers by explaining what they mean in their real-life context.

subject: Math
description: Display numerical data in plots on a number line, including dot or line plots, histograms and box (box and whisker) plots.
description_plain: Your child can show number data on different types of graphs like dot plots, histograms, and box plots.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that a measure of center (mean, median, or mode) for a numerical data set summarizes all of its values with a single number, while a measure of variation (range) describes how its values vary with a single number.
description_plain: Your child understands that one number can show the center of data and another number can show how spread out the data is.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a set of data has a distribution that can be described by its center (mean, median, or mode), spread (range), and overall shape and can be used to answer a statistical question.
description_plain: Your child understands that data has a center, spread, and shape, and can use these to answer questions about the data.

subject: Math
description: Recognize a statistical question as one that anticipates variability in the data related to the question and accounts for it in the answers.
description_plain: Your child knows that a good statistical question expects different answers and includes that in how it is answered.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent two quantities in a real-world problem that change in relationship to one another; write an equation to express one quantity, thought of as the dependent variable, in terms of the other quantity, thought of as the independent variable. Analyze the relationship between the dependent and independent variables using graphs and tables, and relate these to the equation.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to show how two changing amounts relate, write equations for them, and use graphs and tables to understand the relationship.

subject: Math
description: Write an inequality of the form x > c or x < c to represent a constraint or condition in a real-world or mathematical problem. Recognize that inequalities of the form x > c or x < c have infinitely many solutions; represent solutions of such inequalities on number line diagrams.
description_plain: Your child can write inequalities like x > 5 to show limits in problems and draw their solutions on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by writing and solving equations of the form x + p = q and px = q for cases in which p, q and x are all nonnegative rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems by writing and solving simple equations like x + p = q or p × x = q.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent numbers and write expressions when solving a real-world or mathematical problem; understand that a variable can represent an unknown number, or, depending on the purpose at hand, any number in a specified set.
description_plain: Your child can use letters to stand for numbers and write expressions to solve problems, knowing the letter can mean an unknown or any number in a set.

subject: Math
description: Understand solving an equation or inequality as a process of answering a question: which values from a specified set, if any, make the equation or inequality true? Use substitution to determine whether a given number in a specified set makes an equation or inequality true.
description_plain: Your child understands that solving an equation or inequality means finding which numbers make it true and can check by substituting numbers.

subject: Math
description: Identify when two expressions are equivalent (i.e., when the two expressions name the same number regardless of which value is substituted into them).
description_plain: Your child can tell when two math expressions are equal because they give the same answer no matter what number is used.

subject: Math
description: Apply the properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions. Model (e.g., manipulatives, graph paper) and apply the distributive, commutative, identity, and inverse properties with integers and variables by writing equivalent expressions.
description_plain: Your child can use math rules to rewrite expressions in different but equal ways and use models to show this.

subject: Math
description: Evaluate expressions and formulas. Include formulas used in real-world problems. Perform arithmetic operations, including those involving whole number exponents, in the conventional order with or without parentheses. (Order of Operations)
description_plain: Your child can solve math expressions and formulas, including those with exponents and parentheses, like in real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Identify parts of an expression using mathematical terms (sum, term, product, factor, quotient, coefficient); view one or more parts of an expression as a single entity.
description_plain: Your child can name parts of math expressions like sum, product, and factor, and see parts of an expression as one unit.

subject: Math
description: Write expressions that record operations with numbers and with letters standing for numbers.
description_plain: Your child can write math expressions using numbers and letters that stand for numbers.

subject: Math
description: Write, read, and evaluate expressions in which letters stand for numbers.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and solve math expressions that use letters to represent numbers.

subject: Math
description: Write and evaluate numerical expressions involving whole-number exponents.
description_plain: Your child can write and solve math expressions that include whole-number exponents.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems by graphing points in all four quadrants of the coordinate plane. Include use of coordinates and absolute value to find distances between points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems by graphing points in all four parts of the coordinate plane and find distances between points using coordinates and absolute value.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish comparisons of absolute value from statements about order.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between comparing absolute values and comparing the order of numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand the absolute value of a rational number as its distance from 0 on the number line; interpret absolute value as magnitude for a positive or negative quantity in a real-world situation.
description_plain: Your child understands absolute value as how far a number is from zero and can explain it in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Write, interpret, and explain statements of order for rational numbers in real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child can write and explain how numbers compare in order using real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Interpret statements of inequality as statements about the relative position of two numbers on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can understand inequality statements as showing where two numbers are located on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Understand ordering and absolute value of rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child understands how to order rational numbers and what absolute value means.

subject: Math
description: Find and position integers and other rational numbers on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram; find and position pairs of integers and other rational numbers on a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can find and place integers and rational numbers on number lines and coordinate planes.

subject: Math
description: Understand signs of numbers in ordered pairs as indicating locations in quadrants of the coordinate plane; recognize that when two ordered pairs differ only by signs, the locations of the points are related by reflections across one or both axes.
description_plain: Your child understands how the signs of numbers in pairs show locations in different parts of the coordinate plane and how changing signs reflects points across axes.

subject: Math
description: Recognize opposite signs of numbers as indicating locations on opposite sides of 0 on the number line; Recognize that the opposite of the opposite of a number is the number itself [e.g., –(–3) = 3] and that 0 is its own opposite.
description_plain: Your child knows that opposite signs mean numbers are on opposite sides of zero and that the opposite of the opposite number is the original number.

subject: Math
description: Understand a rational number as a point on the number line. Extend number line diagrams and coordinate axes familiar from previous grades to represent points on the line and in the plane with negative number coordinates.
description_plain: Your child understands that rational numbers can be shown as points on number lines and coordinate planes, including negative numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand that positive and negative numbers describe quantities having opposite directions or values (e.g., temperature above/below zero, elevation above/below sea level, credits/debits, positive/negative electric charge); use positive and negative numbers to represent quantities in real-world contexts, explain the meaning of 0 in each situation.
description_plain: Your child understands that positive and negative numbers show opposite directions or values, like temperature above or below zero, and can explain what zero means in these cases.

subject: Math
description: Find the greatest common factor of two whole numbers less than or equal to 100 and the least common multiple of two whole numbers less than or equal to 12. Use the distributive property to express a sum of two whole numbers 1–100 with a common factor as a multiple of a sum of two whole numbers with no common factor.
description_plain: Your child can find the greatest common factor and least common multiple of numbers and use the distributive property to rewrite sums with common factors.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add, subtract, multiply, and divide multi-digit decimals using the standard algorithm for each operation. Express the remainder as a terminating decimal, or a repeating decimal, or rounded to a designated place value.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals using standard methods.

subject: Math
description: Fluently multiply and divide multi-digit whole numbers using the standard algorithm. Express the remainder as a whole number, decimal, or simplified fraction; explain or justify your choice based on the context of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can quickly multiply and divide whole numbers and explain how to show remainders as whole numbers, decimals, or fractions depending on the problem.

subject: Math
description: Interpret and compute quotients of fractions, and solve word problems involving division of fractions by fractions (e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem).
description_plain: Your child can divide fractions by fractions and solve word problems about it using pictures and equations.

subject: Math
description: Use ratio reasoning to convert measurement units between given measurement systems (e.g., convert kilometers to miles); manipulate and transform units appropriately when multiplying or dividing quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use ratios to change measurements between systems, like kilometers to miles, and adjust units when multiplying or dividing.

subject: Math
description: Find a percent of a quantity as a rate per 100 (e.g., 30% of a quantity means 30/100 times the quantity); solve problems involving finding the whole, given a part and the percent.
description_plain: Your child can find a percent of a number and solve problems where you know the part and the percent to find the whole.

subject: Math
description: Solve unit rate problems including those involving unit pricing and constant speed.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems about unit rates, like price per item or speed.

subject: Math
description: Make tables of equivalent ratios relating quantities with whole number measurements, find missing values in the tables, and plot the pairs of values on the coordinate plane. Use tables to compare ratios, and understand equivalencies.
description_plain: Your child can make tables of equivalent ratios, find missing numbers, and graph these pairs to compare ratios.

subject: Math
description: Use ratio and rate reasoning to solve real-world and mathematical problems (e.g., by reasoning about tables of equivalent ratios, tape diagrams, double number line diagrams, or equations).
description_plain: Your child can use ratios and rates to solve real-life and math problems using tables, diagrams, or equations.

subject: Math
description: Understand the concept of a unit rate (a/b associated with a ratio a:b with b ≠ 0, and use rate language in the context of a ratio relationship) and apply it to solve real world problems (e.g., unit pricing, constant speed).
description_plain: Your child understands what a unit rate is and can use it to solve real-world problems like finding prices or speeds.

subject: Math
description: Write and describe the relationship in real life context between two quantities using ratio language.
description_plain: Your child can write and explain how two quantities relate to each other using ratio words.

subject: Math
description: Identify, compare or describe attributes and properties of circles (radius, and diameter). (L)
description_plain: Your child can identify and describe parts of circles like the radius and diameter.

subject: Math
description: Represent three-dimensional figures (e.g., prisms) using nets made up of rectangles and triangles, and use the nets to find the surface area of these figures. Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can draw flat patterns (nets) of 3D shapes like prisms and use them to find surface area in real-life problems.

subject: Math
description: Draw polygons in the coordinate plane given coordinates for the vertices; determine the length of a side joining the coordinates of vertices with the same first or the same second coordinate. Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can draw shapes on a coordinate plane using points and find side lengths when points share an x or y coordinate.

subject: Math
description: Apply the standard formulas to find volumes of prisms. Use the attributes and properties (including shapes of bases) of prisms to identify, compare or describe three-dimensional figures including prisms and cylinders.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the volume of prisms and describe 3D shapes like prisms and cylinders.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of right triangles, other triangles, special quadrilaterals, and polygons by composing or decomposing into other polygons (e.g., rectangles and triangles). Apply these techniques in the context of solving real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of triangles, special four-sided shapes, and other polygons by breaking them into simpler shapes.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional (plane) figures in a hierarchy based on attributes and properties.
description_plain: Your child can sort flat shapes into groups based on their features and properties.

subject: Math
description: Understand that attributes belonging to a category of two-dimensional (plane) figures also belong to all subcategories of that category.
description_plain: Your child understands that if a shape has certain features, all shapes inside that group also have those features.

subject: Math
description: Represent real world and mathematical problems by graphing points in the first quadrant of the coordinate plane, and interpret coordinate values of points in the context of the situation.
description_plain: Your child can plot points on the first quadrant of a coordinate plane and explain what the points mean in real situations.

subject: Math
description: Use a pair of perpendicular number lines, called axes, to define a coordinate system, with the intersection of the lines (the origin) arranged to coincide with the 0 on each line and a given point in the plane located by using an ordered pair of numbers, called its coordinates. Understand that the first number indicates how far to travel from the origin in the direction of one axis, and the second number indicates how far to travel in the direction of the second axis, with the convention that the names of the two axes and the coordinates correspond (e.g., x-axis and x-coordinate, y-axis and y-coordinate).
description_plain: Your child understands how to use two number lines that cross to find and name points using pairs of numbers called coordinates.

subject: Math
description: Solve real world problems involving division of unit fractions by non-zero whole numbers and division of whole numbers by unit fractions (e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem).
description_plain: Your child can solve real problems dividing unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions using pictures and equations.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a whole number by a unit fraction, and compute such quotients.
description_plain: Your child understands and can calculate dividing a whole number by a unit fraction.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a unit fraction by a non-zero whole number, and compute such quotients.
description_plain: Your child understands and can calculate dividing a unit fraction by a whole number.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of division to divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions.
description_plain: Your child can divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions using strategies based on multiplication and division.

subject: Math
description: Solve real world problems involving multiplication of fractions and mixed numbers (e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem).
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life problems by multiplying fractions and mixed numbers, using pictures or equations to help.

subject: Math
description: Explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction greater than 1 results in a product greater than the given number (recognizing multiplication by whole numbers greater than 1 as a familiar case); explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction less than 1 results in a product smaller than the given number; and relating the principle of fraction equivalence a/b = (n × a)/(n × b) to the effect of multiplying a/b by 1. (Division of a fraction by a fraction is not a requirement at this grade.)
description_plain: Your child understands why multiplying by a fraction bigger than 1 makes a number bigger, and multiplying by a fraction smaller than 1 makes it smaller.

subject: Math
description: Comparing the size of a product to the size of one factor on the basis of the size of the other factor, without performing the indicated multiplication.
description_plain: Your child can compare the size of a multiplication answer to one of the numbers being multiplied, without actually multiplying.

subject: Math
description: Interpret multiplication as scaling (resizing), by:
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication as resizing or scaling numbers.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths by tiling it with unit squares of the appropriate unit fraction side lengths, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths. Multiply fractional side lengths to find areas of rectangles, and represent fraction products as rectangular areas.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of rectangles with fractional sides by counting small squares and by multiplying the side lengths.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the product (a/b) × q as a parts of a partition of q into b equal parts; equivalently, as the result of a sequence of operations a × q ÷ b.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplying a fraction by a number as taking parts of that number, using pictures to show this.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction or whole number by a fraction.
description_plain: Your child can multiply fractions and whole numbers by fractions, building on what they already know about multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a fraction as division of the numerator by the denominator (a/b = a ÷ b). Solve word problems involving division of whole numbers leading to answers in the form of fractions or mixed numbers (e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem).
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction means dividing the top number by the bottom number and can solve word problems using this idea.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole, including cases of unlike denominators (e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem). Use benchmark fractions and number sense of fractions to estimate mentally and check the reasonableness of answers.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems adding and subtracting fractions with different denominators, using pictures or equations to help.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract fractions with unlike denominators (including mixed numbers) by replacing given fractions with equivalent fractions in such a way as to produce an equivalent sum or difference of fractions with like denominators.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract fractions with different denominators by changing them to equivalent fractions with the same denominator.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as additive. Find volumes of solid figures composed of two non-overlapping right rectangular prisms by adding the volumes of the non-overlapping parts, applying this technique to solve real world problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of shapes made by joining two rectangular boxes by adding their volumes.

subject: Math
description: Apply the formulas V = l × w × h andV = b × h for rectangular prisms to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with whole number edge lengths in the context of solving real world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the volume of rectangular boxes with whole number sides to solve real problems.

subject: Math
description: Estimate and find the volume of a right rectangular prism with whole-number side lengths by packing it with unit cubes, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths, equivalently by multiplying the height by the area of the base. Demonstrate the associative property of multiplication by using the product of three whole numbers to find volumes (length x width x height).
description_plain: Your child can estimate and find the volume of a rectangular box by filling it with cubes and by multiplying length, width, and height.

subject: Math
description: Relate volume to the operations of multiplication and addition and solve real world and mathematical problems involving volume.
description_plain: Your child understands volume as related to multiplication and addition and can solve problems about volume.

subject: Math
description: Estimate and measure volumes by counting unit cubes, using cubic cm, cubic in, cubic ft, and non-standard units.
description_plain: Your child can estimate and measure volume by counting cubes using different units like cubic centimeters or inches.

subject: Math
description: Estimate and measure volumes by counting unit cubes, using cubic cm, cubic in, cubic ft, and non-standard units.
description_plain: Your child can estimate and measure volume by counting cubes using standard and non-standard units.

subject: Math
description: A solid figure that can be packed without gaps or overlaps using n unit cubes is said to have a volume of n cubic units.
description_plain: Your child understands that the volume of a solid is the number of cubes that fit inside it without gaps or overlaps.

subject: Math
description: A cube with side length 1 unit, called a "unit cube," is said to have "one cubic unit" of volume, and can be used to measure volume.
description_plain: Your child knows that a cube with sides one unit long is called a unit cube and has a volume of one cubic unit.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.
description_plain: Your child understands volume as a property of solid shapes and knows how volume is measured.

subject: Math
description: Explain the classification of data from real-world problems shown in graphical representations including the use of terms mean and median with a given set of data. (L)
description_plain: Your child can explain data shown in graphs and understand terms like mean and median using real-world examples.

subject: Math
description: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (1/2, 1/4, 1/8). Solve problems involving information presented in line plots.
description_plain: Your child can make a line plot to show measurements using fractions and solve problems using the plot.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world problems involving elapsed time between world time zones. (L)
description_plain: Your child can solve real problems involving time differences between different time zones.

subject: Math
description: Identify, estimate measure, and convert equivalent measures within systems English length (inches, feet, yards, miles) weight (ounces, pounds, tons) volume (fluid ounces, cups, pints, quarts, gallons) temperature (Fahrenheit) Metric length (millimeters, centimeters, meters, kilometers) volume (milliliters, liters), temperature (Celsius), (e.g., convert 5 cm to 0.05 m), and use these conversions in solving multi-step, real world problems using appropriate tools.
description_plain: Your child can measure, estimate, and convert between different units of length, weight, volume, and temperature in both English and Metric systems to solve real problems.

subject: Math
description: Add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals to hundredths, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between the operations. Relate the strategy to a written method and explain their reasoning in getting their answers.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals to hundredths using drawings and place value strategies, and explain how they solve these problems.

subject: Math
description: Find whole-number quotients of whole numbers with up to four-digit dividends and two-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, number lines, real life situations, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can divide whole numbers up to four digits by two-digit numbers using place value and multiplication ideas, and explain their work with pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Fluently multiply multi-digit whole numbers using a standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can multiply multi-digit whole numbers quickly and correctly using the standard method.

subject: Math
description: Use place values understanding to round decimals to any place.
description_plain: Your child can round decimals to any place value using their understanding of place values.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to thousandths place based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two decimals up to the thousandths place and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Read and write decimals to thousandths using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form [e.g., 347.392 = 3 x 100 + 4 x 10 + 7 x 1 + 3 (1/10) + 9 (1/100) + 2 (1/1000)].
description_plain: Your child can read and write decimals to the thousandths place in numbers, words, and expanded form.

subject: Math
description: Read, write, and compare decimals to thousandths.
description_plain: Your child can read, write, and compare decimals up to the thousandths place.

subject: Math
description: Explain and extend the patterns in the number of zeros of the product when multiplying a number by powers of 10, and explain and extend the patterns in the placement of the decimal point when a decimal is multiplied or divided by a power of 10. Use whole-number exponents to denote powers of 10.
description_plain: Your child understands patterns when multiplying or dividing by powers of 10 and can explain how the decimal point moves.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that in a multi-digit number, a digit in one place represents 10 times as much as it represents in the place to its right and 1/10 of what it represents in the place to its left.
description_plain: Your child knows that in a number, each digit is ten times bigger than the digit to its right and one-tenth the size of the digit to its left.

subject: Math
description: Generate two numerical patterns using two given rules. Identify apparent relationships between corresponding terms. Form ordered pairs consisting of corresponding terms from the two patterns, and graph the ordered pairs on a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can create two number patterns from rules, find relationships between them, and graph the matching pairs on a coordinate plane.

subject: Math
description: Write simple expressions that record calculations with numbers, and interpret numerical expressions without evaluating them.
description_plain: Your child can write simple math expressions to show calculations and understand what the expressions mean without solving them.

subject: Math
description: Use parentheses to construct numerical expressions, and evaluate numerical expressions with these symbols.
description_plain: Your child can use parentheses in math expressions and correctly solve expressions that include them.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like calculators, rulers, or graphs, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and question the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Design and use a simulation to generate frequencies for compound events.
description_plain: Your child can create and use simulations to find how often combined events happen.

subject: Math
description: Represent sample spaces for compound events using methods such as organized lists, tables and tree diagrams. For an event described in everyday language (e.g., "rolling double sixes"), identify the outcomes in the sample space which compose the event.
description_plain: Your child can list all possible outcomes for combined events using organized methods and identify which outcomes match a described event.

subject: Math
description: Understand that, just as with simple events, the probability of a compound event is the fraction of outcomes in the sample space for which the compound event occurs.
description_plain: Your child understands that the chance of combined events happening is the fraction of all possible outcomes that fit the event.

subject: Math
description: Find probabilities of compound events using organized lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulation.
description_plain: Your child can find the probability of combined events using lists, tables, tree diagrams, and simulations.

subject: Math
description: Design a probability model (which may not be uniform) by observing frequencies in data generated from a chance process.
description_plain: Your child can create a probability model by watching how often things happen in chance experiments, even if the chances are not all equal.

subject: Math
description: Design a uniform probability model by assigning equal probability to all outcomes, and use the model to determine probabilities of events.
description_plain: Your child can create a simple probability model where all outcomes are equally likely and use it to find the chance of different events.

subject: Math
description: Develop a probability model and use it to find probabilities of events. Compare probabilities from a model to observed frequencies; if the agreement is not good, explain possible sources of the discrepancy.
description_plain: Your child can build a probability model to find chances of events and compare these to actual results, explaining why they might be different.

subject: Math
description: Approximate the probability of a chance event by collecting data on the chance process that produces it and observing its long-run relative frequency, and predict the approximate relative frequency given the probability.
description_plain: Your child can estimate the chance of an event by collecting data and observing how often it happens over time, then predict future chances.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the probability of a chance event is a number between 0 and 1 that expresses the likelihood of the event occurring. Larger numbers indicate greater likelihood. A probability near 0 indicates an unlikely event, a probability around 1/2 indicates an event that is neither unlikely nor likely, and a probability near 1 indicates a likely event.
description_plain: Your child understands that probability is a number from 0 to 1 showing how likely an event is, with numbers near 0 meaning unlikely and near 1 meaning very likely.

subject: Math
description: Use measures of center and measures of variability for numerical data from random samples to draw informal comparative inferences about two populations.
description_plain: Your child can use averages and spread of data from random samples to make simple comparisons between two groups.

subject: Math
description: Informally assess the degree of visual overlap of two numerical data distributions with similar variabilities, measuring the difference between the centers by expressing it as a multiple of a measure of variability.
description_plain: Your child can look at two sets of data and estimate how much their values overlap and how different their centers are compared to their spread.

subject: Math
description: Use data from a random sample to draw inferences about a population with an unknown characteristic of interest. Generate multiple samples (or simulated samples) of the same size to gauge the variation in estimates or predictions.
description_plain: Your child can use data from random samples to make guesses about a larger group and create several samples to see how much the guesses might change.

subject: Math
description: Understand that statistics can be used to gain information about a population by examining a reasonably sized sample of the population; generalizations about a population from a sample are valid only if the sample is representative of that population. Understand that random sampling tends to produce representative samples and support valid inferences.
description_plain: Your child understands that looking at a good, random sample of a group can help learn about the whole group, but the sample must fairly represent it.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to inequalities of the form px + q > r or px + q < r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. Graph the solution set of the inequality and interpret it in the context of the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that lead to inequalities like px + q > r, graph the solutions, and explain what the graph means for the problem.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems leading to equations of the form px + q = r and p(x + q) = r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that lead to equations like px + q = r or p(x + q) = r, solve them easily, and compare algebra and arithmetic methods.

subject: Math
description: Use variables to represent quantities in a real-world or mathematical problem, and construct multi-step equations and inequalities to solve problems by reasoning about the quantities.
